one_line_takeaway: Interleaving a harmful request with benign auxiliary tasks (optionally with character reversal or shaped-output constraints) both raises jailbreak success rates against thinking-mode LLMs beyond six existing baselines and, uniquely, induces measurable reasoning failures (up to 17% thinking-collapse rate and 60% response-repetition rate on Qwen3 4B) that content-safety-only attacks do not cause.
key_finding: MS_Reverse is the strongest of the three strategies and outperforms all six baselines on ASR across JailbreakBench, AdvBench, and HarmBench, in both standard and thinking mode, on the Qwen3 series (1.7B/4B/8B); on JailbreakBench it achieves the highest ASR on DeepSeek (standard and thinking mode) and highest thinking-mode ASR on Qwen3-Max, ranking near top on Gemini 2.5 Flash, with the conclusion citing ASR exceeding 90% on certain models. Beyond content-safety bypass, MSPA induces reasoning-specific failures not seen with baselines: thinking collapse rate (TCR) up to 17% on Qwen3 4B (MS_Reverse) vs. near 0% for baselines, and response repetition rate (RRR) up to 60% on Qwen3 4B (25% on DeepSeek) vs. below 2% for other methods. Thinking length under MS_Reverse exceeds 10K characters on Qwen3 8B and over 20K characters on DeepSeek (Table 1: DeepSeek MS_Reverse harmful-response thinking length 17734±1209 characters; MS_Structure reaches 28321±537 on DeepSeek), versus 2K-4K characters typical for baseline attacks. Thinking time for MS_Reverse reaches up to 7 minutes and MS_Structure up to 9 minutes on DeepSeek. Ablation shows ASR is lower on the 1.7B model, rises sharply at 4B, then dips slightly at 8B (attributed to stronger safety alignment at larger scale). Against harmfulness detectors, Qwen3Guard 4B is the best-performing defense, reaching 84.08% accuracy / 5.31% FPR / 9.50% FNR on the challenging (358-sample) set and 92.22% accuracy / 4.58% FPR / 3.19% FNR on the regular (720-sample) set; keyword-based detection only reaches 60.89% accuracy on the challenging set; Llama-Prompt-Guard-2 variants perform worst (49.16% and 41.34% accuracy for the 22M and 86M variants respectively). The paper also reports that 36.31% of Qwen-series and 16.36% of DeepSeek harmful responses under the attack self-justify as 'educational purposes.'
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is 'multi-stream perturbation attack' (MSPA), a black-box jailbreak method specifically targeting thinking-mode (reasoning) LLMs. It interleaves a harmful task word-by-word with one or more benign auxiliary tasks inside a single prompt, using three concrete perturbation strategies: (1) MS (multi-stream interleaving) — interleaves the harmful task with benign tasks marked by different delimiters such as {·} and [·]; (2) MS_Reverse (inversion perturbation) — additionally reverses each auxiliary word at the character level to increase decoding burden; (3) MS_Structure (shape transformation) — adds a triangular output-format constraint (line i must contain i characters) on top of MS. The authors frame the mechanism as forcing the model to parse multiple interleaved semantic paths simultaneously, which disperses the attention/computation available for safety detection and breaks the sequential integrity that safety filters rely on to judge harmfulness.
threat_model: Black-box attacker with only API/chat access to a target LLM operating in 'thinking mode' (explicit chain-of-thought reasoning before the final answer), no access to model weights or gradients. The attacker submits a single perturbed prompt (harmful task interleaved with benign auxiliary tasks, optionally with character reversal and/or format constraints) and aims to either (a) elicit a harmful response that bypasses safety alignment, or (b) induce reasoning-process failure (thinking collapse or repetitive/looping output). Targets evaluated: open-source Qwen3 1.7B/4B/8B (both standard and thinking mode) and API-based DeepSeek, Qwen3-Max, and Gemini 2.5 Flash.
limitations: Single-author paper with a code link to an anonymous repository (not yet an established public artifact at review time). Baseline count is internally inconsistent in the text ('five representative jailbreak attack methods' is stated in Section 4.1 but six are then named and used, including JAIL-CON and FlipAttack). Evaluation excludes OpenAI o1 and Grok because their thinking traces are inaccessible/post-processed, so generalization to those closed reasoning models is untested. Judgment of 'attack success' (ASR) and detection ground truth rely on the authors' own evaluation pipeline (e.g., using GPT-4/DeepSeek/Qwen3Guard as judges), which is a common but imperfect practice in jailbreak literature; the truncated text obtained here does not include the specific ASR judge methodology (likely detailed further in the appendix, not fully captured in this extraction). TCR/RRR effects are highly model-dependent — MS_Reverse and MS_Structure barely affect DeepSeek's repetition rate (0-2%) while strongly affecting Qwen3 series (up to 60%), so the 'reasoning collapse' finding does not generalize uniformly across architectures. The mechanistic explanation (attention dispersion, cognitive-resource competition) is argued analogically (citing cocktail-party-effect and human information-processing-rate literature) rather than verified via internal model diagnostics (e.g., attention weights, activation analysis).

datasets_used: Custom 358-sample 'challenging' harmfulness-detection evaluation set (role-play/ambiguous harmful content)
quantitative_results: MS_Reverse ASR exceeds 90% on certain models (per Conclusion)
